worth_it_explanation: Curated packages are how a publisher's inventory reaches buyers as a named, targetable product through Index Marketplace rather than as undifferentiated open-auction supply. A package that points at the wrong inventory, or that carries audience and content signals that no longer match what the deal promised, sends a buyer impressions they did not agree to buy, which erodes the deal's performance and the publisher's standing with that buyer. The check is a review of curated packages a publisher already has in place, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal or mapping the publisher can correct.
intro: Curation packages the publisher's inventory into named deals that buyers can target through Index Marketplace, often layered with audience or content signals so a buyer reaches a specific slice of supply at an agreed price. This subcategory is about whether those curated packages are set up against the right inventory and kept current, rather than the open-auction pricing that governs unpackaged supply. These checks confirm that each curated deal maps to the inventory and signals it was meant to carry, and that packages are reviewed rather than left to drift as the inventory and the audiences behind them change.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are mapped to the wrong inventory or signals
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated deal that targets inventory or audience signals the buyer did not agree to buy delivers impressions outside the deal's terms, which depresses the deal's measured performance and can cost the publisher the buyer's spend. The mismatch is a configuration error rather than a market condition, so it sits unseen until the buyer notices the deal is underdelivering against what it promised.

educate.base:
A curated deal is a packaged product. The publisher names a slice of inventory, often layers an audience segment or a content signal on top, attaches a price, and exposes it to a buyer as a deal ID the buyer can target. When the buyer activates that deal ID, the bid request that reaches them is supposed to carry exactly the inventory and the signals the deal described. The whole value of curation rests on that promise holding because the buyer is paying a negotiated price for a defined product rather than bidding into the open auction.

The mapping between the deal and the inventory is where this breaks. A package can point at an ad unit group that has since changed, at a site that was meant to be excluded, or at an audience segment that no longer carries the data it did when the deal was built. In each case the deal ID still resolves and bids still clear, so nothing looks broken from the publisher's side. The buyer, however, receives impressions that fall outside what they agreed to buy, and the deal's performance reflects supply the buyer never wanted.

In a header-bidding setup, the signals a curated deal relies on travel as first-party data and key-value pairs on the bid request, and the deal itself surfaces to the ad server as a deal ID carried in targeting. The audit question is whether each curated package resolves to the inventory and the signals it was defined against, or whether the mapping has drifted from what the buyer was sold.

A few signs point to a package mapped to the wrong inventory or signals:
  • A curated deal targets an ad unit group or site list that no longer matches the inventory description the buyer agreed to
  • The audience or content signal attached to a package no longer reflects the segment the deal was built around
  • A buyer reports the deal delivering impressions from placements or audiences outside the deal's stated terms
You may want to confirm that each curated package resolves to the inventory and signals it was defined against. One approach is to trace a sample of active deals from the package definition through to the bid requests they appear on, and compare what the deal carries against what the buyer was told it would.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a curated package often promises a specific content context, for example a deal scoped to a single vertical or a sponsorship-adjacent section. You may want to confirm that the content signal on each package still maps to the sections it names, given that a site restructure or a section rename can leave a deal pointing at content that no longer exists or no longer fits the buyer's brief.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a curated package may promise a high-intent audience, for example shoppers on product or category pages. You may want to confirm that the audience signal attached to each package still reflects that intent, given that a change to how the segment is built can leave the deal carrying a broader or staler audience than the buyer agreed to.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed configuration documentation for setting up and mapping curated packages in Index Marketplace is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each active curated package against its definition, tracing the inventory and signals it targets to what the buyer was told the deal would carry.

ix_ui.label: Index Exchange Marketplace UI
steps: Open the curated deals or Index Marketplace section and list the active curated packages with their deal IDs.
For each package, open it and record the inventory it targets, any audience or content signal attached, and the price.
Compare each package's targeting against the inventory description and signals the buyer agreed to, and note any package where they no longer match.
Ask the publisher whether any curated deal has been flagged by a buyer for delivering supply outside its terms, and record the deal and the issue reported.

prebid_config.label: Prebid configuration and ad server
steps: Review the first-party data and key-value pairs the header-bidding setup passes, and confirm the signals a curated deal depends on are present on the bid requests for the inventory the deal names.
Check the ad server line items keyed to each deal ID and confirm they target the inventory and priority the deal was meant to carry.
Note any case where the deal ID resolves but the signals or inventory behind it differ from the package definition.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, the inventory and signals it targets and whether they match the description the buyer agreed to. A package whose inventory and signals resolve to what the deal was defined against passes. A package targeting an ad unit group, site list, or audience signal that no longer matches the buyer's agreed terms is a fail recorded with the deal ID and the dimension where the mapping diverged.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the inventory and signals each curated package targets and how they compare to the buyer's agreed terms above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more curated packages on {{site_name}} map to inventory or audience signals that no longer match what the buyer agreed to buy. A curated deal is sold as a defined product, so when the deal ID resolves to the wrong ad units, sites, or signals, the buyer receives impressions outside the deal's terms and the deal underdelivers against what it promised. {{voice_recommend}} tracing each active curated package from its definition through to the bid requests and ad server line items it appears on, then correcting any package whose inventory or signals have drifted from the agreed terms. After the correction, confirm with the affected buyer that the deal now delivers the supply it was sold as.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are left unmaintained as inventory and demand change
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Curated packages built once and never reviewed drift as inventory is added or retired and as buyer demand shifts, so a package can keep running on a shrinking or inactive deal while newer inventory goes unpackaged. The cost is quiet underdelivery and missed revenue rather than an outright failure, which is why a stale package can sit unreviewed for a long stretch.

educate.base:
A curated package reflects the inventory and the demand that existed when it was built. Both move. A publisher adds new sections, retires old placements, restructures ad units, and refreshes the audience segments behind a package. Buyers shift their spend, pause deals, and renegotiate terms. A package that is set up once and then left alone slowly stops describing the inventory and the demand it was meant to bridge.

The drift shows up in two directions. A package can keep targeting inventory that has shrunk or been retired, so the deal carries thinner supply than the buyer expected, or it can miss newer inventory that should have been folded in, so valuable impressions stay in the open auction instead of clearing through a higher-priced deal. On the demand side, a deal can sit active long after the buyer stopped spending against it, which clutters the package list and obscures which deals are actually carrying revenue.

Index Marketplace exposes the activity behind each curated package, including which deals are bidding, how much they deliver, and which sit idle. The audit question is whether the publisher reviews packages on a regular cadence against current inventory and demand, or whether the package list has accumulated deals that no longer reflect what the inventory and the buyers are doing now.

A few signs point to packages that are not maintained:
  • Curated deals exist with no recorded review since they were created, even as inventory has been added or retired
  • The package list carries deals that show little or no delivery, which suggests the buyer has moved on
  • Newer or higher-value inventory is not represented in any curated package, so it clears only in the open auction
You may want to confirm that curated packages are reviewed on a regular cadence rather than built once and forgotten. One approach is to read delivery by deal, retire or refresh the packages that no longer carry meaningful spend, and check whether current inventory is represented where a curated deal would price it better than the open auction.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, editorial coverage and section structure change often, so a curated package scoped to a vertical or a content theme can fall out of step within a season. You may want to review content-scoped packages whenever sections are added or reorganized, so each deal still reaches the content it was built to package.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the product range and the seasons behind it shift, and an audience package built around one set of high-intent pages can go stale as the catalog changes. You may want to review audience-scoped packages on a cadence that tracks the catalog, so the curated deals still reach the shopper intent the buyer is paying for.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's reporting on curated deal delivery and the controls for editing or retiring packages in Index Marketplace are in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the curated package list against current inventory and recent delivery to see which packages still reflect the inventory and demand they were built for.

ix_ui.label: Index Exchange Marketplace UI
steps: Open the curated deals or Index Marketplace section and list every active curated package with its creation or last-edit date.
For each package, note its recent delivery and whether the buyer is still bidding against it, so idle deals stand out.
Compare the inventory each package targets against the publisher's current ad unit and site structure, and note any package pointing at retired inventory or missing newer inventory.
Record which packages have no review on file and which carry little or no delivery, so they can be refreshed or retired.

ix_reporting.label: Index Exchange reporting
steps: Build a view of delivery by curated deal, with impressions, revenue, and the buyer behind each.
Compare delivery across packages to see which deals carry meaningful spend and which have gone idle.
Note any high-value inventory that does not appear in any curated package, which points to supply that a deal could price above the open auction.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, its last review date, its recent delivery, and whether its targeted inventory matches the current structure. Packages reviewed on a recent cadence that still carry delivery and target current inventory pass. Packages with no recent review, little or no delivery, or targeting that points at retired inventory or misses newer inventory are a fail recorded with the deal IDs and the way each has drifted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each curated package's last review date, recent delivery, and whether its targeting matches current inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that curated packages on {{site_name}} are running without a regular review, so some target inventory that has been retired while newer inventory stays unpackaged and a few deals sit idle with little delivery. Curated packages reflect the inventory and demand that existed when they were built, and both move, so a package left unmaintained slowly stops describing what the inventory and the buyers are doing now. {{voice_recommend}} reading delivery by curated deal, retiring or refreshing the packages that no longer carry meaningful spend, and folding current high-value inventory into a package where a deal would price it better than the open auction. After the review, set a recurring cadence so the packages stay aligned with the inventory and demand behind them.
